coredata.dc:title: Transcriptomic response and perturbation of toxicity pathways in zebrafish larvae after exposure to graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
coredata.dc:creator: Deng, Shun
Jia, Pan-Pan
Zhang, Jing-Hui
Junaid, Muhammad
Niu, Aping
Ma, Yan-Bo
Fu, Ailing
Pei, De-Sheng
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely used for biomedical applications. Previously, the low-level toxicity of GQDs in vivo and in vitro has been elucidated, but the underlying molecular mechanisms remained largely unknown. Here, we employed the Illumina high-throughput RNA-sequencing to explore the whole-transcriptome profiling of zebrafish larvae after exposure to GQDs. Comparative transcriptome analysis identified 2116 differentially expressed genes between GQDs exposed groups and control. Functional classification demonstrated that a large proportion of genes involved in acute inflammatory responses and detoxifying process were significantly up-regulated by GQDs. The inferred gene regulatory network suggested that activator protein 1 (AP-1) was the early-response transcription factor in the linkage of a cascade of downstream (pro-) inflammatory signals with the apoptosis signals. Moreover, hierarchical signaling threshold determined the high sensitivity of complement system in zebrafish when exposed to the sublethal dose of GQDs. Further, 35 candidate genes from various signaling pathways were further validated by qPCR after exposure to 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL of GQDs. Taken together, our study provided a valuable insight into the molecular mechanisms of potential bleeding risks and detoxifying processes in response to GQDs exposure, thereby establishing a mechanistic basis for the biosafety evaluation of GQDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Key words
GQDs
Signaling pathways
Transcriptome analysis
Zebrafish

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: GQDs preparation and imaging in zebrafish larvae
Functional classification of differentially expressed genes
GQDs induced AP-1-dependent regulation of il-8 signaling pathway
GQDs activated the redox-sensitive signaling pathway
GQDs triggered signaling pathways in a concentration-dependent manner
RNA extraction, library preparation and Illumina Hiseq4000 sequencing
Differential expression analysis and functional enrichment

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Hundred Talents Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences
National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful for the supports from the Three Hundred Leading Talents in Scientific and Technological Innovation Program of Chongqing (No. CSTCCXLJRC201714 to D.S.P.), the Key Application and Development Program of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (No. cstc2014yykfC20004 and cstc2014yykfC20002 to D.S.P), the Hundred Talents Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (to D.S.P.), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21407146 to Y.B.M. and No. 51641907 to A.P.N). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and in vivo fluorescence imaging of GQDs. (A) TEM image and (B) the size distribution of GQDs (scale bar = 20 nm). (C) Images of the as-synthesized GQDs under white (left) and ultraviolet light (right), respectively. (D) Images of zebrafish larvae treated with a gradient concentration (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL) of GQDs, and the fluorescence excited by GQDs in zebrafish larvae (72 hpf) was observed with the fluorescence microscope in bright field, UV (330–380 nm) and blue (460–490 nm) fluorescence field (scale bar = 1000 μm) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

GO subcategories and the enriched KEGG pathways. (A) Summary of the numbers of up- and down-regulated DEGs for each GO subcategory. (B) The presentation of 18 significantly enriched KEGG pathways, and the related pathways involving in acute inflammatory responses, metabolic homeostasis, and blood pressure were well generalized according to p value < 0.05 and the corrected p value (FDR) < 0.05. Heatmap for metabolic enzymes were exhibited between Controls (E3 solution) and GQDs exposed zebrafish, values were represented by log10 (fold changes).

qPCR verification and gene expression profiles in various signaling pathways in zebrafish after exposure to a gradient concentration of GQDs (0-25-50-100 μg/mL). (A) Transcriptional levels of DEGs were normalized to the internal control gene (egfɑ), gene expression changes for qPCR and RNA-seq were indicated by X-axis and Y-axis, respectively. R-squared values (R2) for all tested genes were figured out with Pearson correlation coefficient and the P value between the two groups was calculated by Independent-Samples t-test, note: only the differentially regulated genes were selected to be analyzed. (B) qPCR for the gene expression profiles in zebrafish exposed to a gradient concentration of GQDs (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL), and the dashed box with different colors expressed the different signaling pathways triggered by GQDs, the comparison of gene expression levels between GQDs and controls was statistically performed by Independent-Samples t-test, * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001. (C) A brief visualization of hierarchical gene responses in innate immune defense systems and associated signaling pathways in response to a gradient concentration of GQDs (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL).

Apoptosis detection of GQDs exposed zebrafish embryos at 48 hpf. (A) Apoptosis imaging of zebrafish embryos under 10 × CLSM when exposed a concentration dependence of GQDs (0, 25, 50, 100, 200 μg/mL). (B) Statistics of GQDs induced apoptosis in zebrafish embryos exposed to a gradient concentration (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL) of GQDs. Data were analyzed by Independent-Samples t-test,* p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01.

ROS induced fluorescent variation in GQDs exposed zebrafish embryos at 72 hpf, all values were indicated by means ± SD from 3 independent biological replicates, 35 embryos per treatment. Data were analyzed by Independent-Samples t-test,* p < 0.05.

Gene regulatory network deduced from the combined data (RNA-seq and qPCR results), and genes names were represented by orthologous human protein name. pd: protein-DNA interaction, pp: protein-protein interaction, and the Heatmap showed the log2 (fold changes) of these genes in RNA-seq data. Briefly, AP-1 complex and its components (c-Jun, c-Fos, JDP2, ATF2, and ATF3) were all described on the map.
Primers used for quantitative real-time PCR in this study.
Omics profiling highlights the key pathways related to the cytotoxicity or genotoxicity induced by Carbon NPs.

row.entry:
Involved Key GO/KEGG pathways
(≥ 3 were listed)
Hepatic fibrosis/Hepatic stellate cell activation, AhR signaling, Superpathway of cholesterol synthesis, etc.
Acute inflammatory and Fibrotic responses, Cell cycle arrest, Cell proliferation inhibition, Cell apoptosis
AHR, c-FOS, PTGS2, NFE2L2, CEBPA, etc.
Gluconeogenesis, Glucose metabolism, Metabolism of carbohydrates, T-cell receptor and co-stimulatory signaling, etc.
Cytoskeleton remodeling, Membrane trafficking/exocytosis, Stress responsive proteins
NF-kβ, Nitric oxide (NO), and NAPDH oxidase signaling, etc.
Cell apoptosis, Cell cycle arrest, DNA replication stress, and Genome instability
NF-kβ, p300, c-JUN, CYP1 A1, etc.
HSP-1, α- glucosidase AB, Msh2, DJ-1, etc.
HMG-CoA reductase pathway, LXL/RXR activation, Glutathione-mediated detoxification, etc.
Phosphorylation/metabolism, Hemopoiesis, and Chemotactic pathways, etc.
Acute inflammatory and Fibrotic responses, Cell apoptosis
IL-12, S100a8, S100a9, ApoA1, AT111, etc.
JNK signaling, AhR signaling, Interleukin signaling pathway, etc.
JNK, Insulin, apoptosis and DNA damage signaling pathways, etc.
Palmitic acid, octadecanoic acid, uracil, etc.

head.ce:title: Transcriptomic response and perturbation of toxicity pathways in zebrafish larvae after exposure to graphene quantum dots (GQDs)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chongqing 400714, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 100049, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: University of Chinese Academy of Sciences

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: College of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Southwest University, Chongqing 400715, China; Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Chongqing 400714, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are widely used for biomedical applications. Previously, the low-level toxicity of GQDs in vivo and in vitro has been elucidated, but the underlying molecular mechanisms remained largely unknown. Here, we employed the Illumina high-throughput RNA-sequencing to explore the whole-transcriptome profiling of zebrafish larvae after exposure to GQDs. Comparative transcriptome analysis identified 2116 differentially expressed genes between GQDs exposed groups and control. Functional classification demonstrated that a large proportion of genes involved in acute inflammatory responses and detoxifying process were significantly up-regulated by GQDs. The inferred gene regulatory network suggested that activator protein 1 (AP-1) was the early-response transcription factor in the linkage of a cascade of downstream (pro-) inflammatory signals with the apoptosis signals. Moreover, hierarchical signaling threshold determined the high sensitivity of complement system in zebrafish when exposed to the sublethal dose of GQDs. Further, 35 candidate genes from various signaling pathways were further validated by qPCR after exposure to 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL of GQDs. Taken together, our study provided a valuable insight into the molecular mechanisms of potential bleeding risks and detoxifying processes in response to GQDs exposure, thereby establishing a mechanistic basis for the biosafety evaluation of GQDs.

ce:section.ce:para: The emergence of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) has recently attracted widespread attention for their great potentials in biomedical applications, due to their unique physicochemical properties such as excellent photothermal stability, tunable photoluminescence (PL), high optoelectronic sensitivity and biocompatibility [1,2] (Wang, 2010 #179). GQDs are derived from graphene (G), which is a single-atom-thick crystal characterized by sp2-bonded carbons, arranged in a honeycomb lattice [3,4]. GQDs inherit many structural and functional features from G, but the smaller size, higher crystallinity, and wider PL bandwidth make them to be one of the most preferable nanoparticles (NPs) in bioengineering applications such as bioimaging, drug carriers, optical sensing, and theranostics [5,6]. Previous pioneering work showed that GQDs incorporated fluorescent labels exhibited exceptional attributes in the real-time molecular monitoring of cell motility and drug delivery [7–9]. Meanwhile, urgent concerns on the nanotoxicity of GQDs raised, because the potential risks of GQDs to human health and ecosystem were previously unknown [10–12].

Although most of in vivo and in vitro studies of the bio-distribution and toxicity assessments suggested that GQDs have low cytotoxicity and genotoxicity, even at relatively higher exposure concentrations [13–18], there were few reports on the developmental disturbance in animals after GQDs exposure to concentrations above an acceptable level [19]. Previous studies revealed that GQDs and other carbon-based nanomaterials could activate a series of similar immune responses, biochemical and metabolic activities through inflammatory cells and immune-associated genes related to various signaling pathways [16,20]. However, the underlying molecular mechanisms remained elusive, especially the perturbation of toxicity pathways induced by these nanoparticles is still a mystery.

Zebrafish has proven as a preferred model organism for toxicity testing because of its small size, low maintenance cost, optical transparency, and convenient manipulation [21–23]. Zebrafish genome shares many homologs with the human genome, which are also highly conserved between zebrafish and other vertebrate animals. Moreover, zebrafish at the early life stage is very sensitive to monitor environmental pollutants due to the uptake of waterborne toxicants [24,25]. Previously, zebrafish was used to evaluate the toxicity of graphene oxide (GO) [26], carbon nanotubes [27], carbon nanodots [28], and other carbon nanoparticles [29–31]. However, most of the past research mainly focused on the acute toxicological effects and related molecular apoptosis mechanisms, and the potential chronic injuries caused by NPs and associated molecular signaling pathways were seldom studied. Recently, the introduction of high-throughput screening approaches to evaluate NPs’ toxicity unveil the gene networks underlying the multidimensionally toxicological effects on in vivo and in vitro experimental systems. Besides ROS generation and mitochondrial dysfunction, the earlier studies also highlighted the toxicity potentials of CNTs and GO using high-throughput transcriptome and proteomic sequencing analysis [26,32–34]. Previous studies revealed specific perturbations in mitochondrial membrane potential (MMP), acute inflammatory responses, metabolic homeostasis, and chronic inflammations remarkably triggered by CNTs and GO exposure involving various receptor proteins such as c-FOS, cyclooxygenase 2 (COX2), amyloid precursor protein (APP), nuclear respiratory factor 1 (NRF1), mitogen-activated protein kinase 8 (JNK1), Immunoglobulin G (IgG), Integrins, and several pivotal miRNA/lncRNA (Table 2
). Although, the studies those highlighted the toxicity mechanisms of pristine CNTs and GO are existing, the molecular mechanisms of GQDs toxicity especially on in vivo systems remain largely unknown. However, it has been revealed recently that GQDs share some commonly reported signaling pathways with other carbon-based NPs [16,35].

In this study, high-throughput sequencing (HTS) was firstly applied to investigate the molecular toxicity mechanisms of GQDs in zebrafish larvae. Zebrafish larvae were exposed to a relatively higher GQDs concentration (100 μg/mL) for 7 d, and the total RNAs of both GQDs treated groups and control were extracted to generate cDNA libraries for Illumina Hiseq4000 sequencing. GO and KEGG annotations with assembled differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were performed, and subsequently, GQDs induced DEGs and associated signaling pathways were generalized. Furthermore, to verify the results of sequencing, qPCRs were applied to check the expression levels of some DEGs related to different signaling pathways after zebrafish larvae exposed to a gradient concentration of GQDs (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs preparation and imaging in zebrafish larvae
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 A, the transmission electron microscope (TEM) was used to detect the distribution and structure of GQDs. The result showed that the sizes of GQDs nanoparticles were 1–9 nm, in which 3–6 nm nanoparticles occupied about 80% of the synthesized GQDs (Fig. 1B), and further details about the GQDs chemical characterization was discussed in our previous paper [36]. GQDs dissolved in distilled water displayed yellowish brown color under the white light and pale yellow fluorescence under the ultraviolet irradiation (Fig. 1C), indicating the soluble stability of GQDs in water. To observe the body uptake of GQDs in zebrafish larvae, zebrafish were exposed to GQDs solution, and the result showed that GQDs exposed larvae exhibited fluorescence in a concentration-dependent manner (Fig. 1D).

RNA-seq transcriptome libraries were constructed according to the TruSeqTM RNA sample preparation Kit from Illumina (San Diego, CA, USA). Raw paired-end reads (PE) were sequenced with the Illumina HiSeq 4000 platform (2 × 150 bp read length). In brief, 192, 106, 288 and 159, 375, 370 raw PE reads were acquired from GQDs exposed and control groups, and three biological replicates were pooled for each group. Raw data were quality controlled by discarding adaptor reads, the reads with unknown nucleotides larger than 10%, and the low quality reads (Q30% > 95%). Finally, 187,981,522 and 156,434,896 clean PE reads were obtained in GQDs and controls, respectively. Clean reads were separately aligned to reference zebrafish genome (Zv9) and assembled by using TopHat 2.1.1 version. Average 78.5% and 78.31% clean reads of each sample were uniquely mapped into the known genome sequences (Fig. S1A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functional classification of differentially expressed genes
ce:section.ce:para: Totally, 31,953 transcripts were finally mined from all clean reads, and FPKM abundance and magnitude distribution for each transcript were represented by scatter-plot and volcano-plots, respectively (Fig. S1B and Fig. S1C). DEGs were further identified with DESeq2 packages in the R software [37]. DEGs with 1.5 fold or more changes between GQDs and control groups were discriminated upon the criteria of the corrected p value (FDR) < 0.05. An amount of 2116 DEGs were statistically classified from the assembled transcripts, including 1102 up-regulated DEGs and 1014 down-regulated DEGs. Functions of these DEGs were annotated by reference to the Ensembl zebrafish database (Zv9 Ensemble release 79), as well as supplemented with the description of homologous results by using a blastx search tool against public protein database, such as Non-redundant (Nr), Swiss-prot, and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG). Conventionally, gene ontology (GO) enrichment was performed to reveal these DEGs’ functional classification. Then, 912 up-regulated DEGs and 818 down-regulated DEGs were described into three main divisions, including biological process, cellular component and molecular function. The biological process cluster was further classified into 25 subcategories, cellular component into 18 subcategories, and molecular function into 15 subcategories (Fig. 2 A). The subcategories of “cellular process”, “single-organism transport”, and “metabolic process” were the three most abundant GO terms within the biological process category, to which a very high percentage of annotated DEGs were assigned. To a lesser extent, the subdivisions of “membrane part”, “integral component of membrane”, “catalytic activity”, and “ion binding” were the dominant GO terms in other two clusters.

To better characterize the GO-annotated DEGs in the zebrafish larvae treated with GQDs, DEGs were functionally sorted into the canonical signaling pathways based on KEGG pathways analysis. A total of 1425 DEGs (797 up-regulated and 628 down-regulated) were matched to the known pathway maps and constructed as 251 KEGG pathways, and 398 DEGs were classified into 18 statistically significant categories (p < 0.05, Fig. 2B). Upon the corrected p value (FDR) < 0.05, 115 KEGG-annotated DEGs (28.9%) were significantly enriched into three main pathways: complement and coagulation cascades (31), Lysosome (53), and TNF signaling pathway (31), in which most of the DEGs tightly correlated with acute phase responses, cell apoptosis, and innate inflammatory responses were significantly up-regulated by GQDs. Next, we found that GQDs can disrupt gene expression related to metabolic homeostasis, at least five prominent metabolic activities governed by 11 KEGG metabolic pathways (45 specific enzymes) were identified to be stimulated by GQDs, respectively (acetylation 8↑, 9↓; sialylation 7↓; galactosylation 8↑, 5↓; methylation 4↑, 5↓; calcium ion activities 7↑). In addition, RAS system was found to be exceptionally activated by GQDs, suggesting that GQDs can activate zebrafish vascular system and cause related disorders.

A relatively wide range of genes involved in the acute inflammatory responses were identified to be significantly induced by GQDs and most of them belonged to the plasma cascade system (Table S1). In the complement system, seven complement components were notably up-regulated, including complement c3a, c3b, c5a, c7, c8a, c8g, and c9. The cascaded effects suggested that the membrane attack complex (MAC) was activated by GQDs. In addition, the complement factor B (cfb) and factor H (cfh) were both found to be significantly up-regulated, indicating the specific triggering of C3b-initiated alternative pathway by GQDs. A hierarchical ensemble of coagulation factors was also remarkably aroused, including coagulation factor V (fⅤ), VII (fⅦ), IX (fⅨ), thrombin (fⅡ), fibrinogen (fⅠ) and plasminogen (plg). All of them were exclusively grouped into the tissue factor pathway of coagulation cascade. Thrombin generation is theoretically derived from the successive activation of multiple coagulation factors, which is initially from fⅦ [38]. The high expression levels of thrombin and fibrinogen genes implied a highly frequent conversion of fibrinogen to fibrin, which is a marker protein of blood clotting [39]. The parallel activation of c3, c5, fⅨ, fⅡ, and plg raised the possibility that FⅨ and thrombin might directly act on the complement system through the proteolytic cleavage of C3 and C5, which had been well verified in previous studies [40]. Meanwhile, a considerable number of cathepsin proteases in commitment to acute phase response, and cell apoptosis were significantly induced by GQDs. Ten cathepsin genes were up-regulated by more than 2 fold, compared to control, and the transcripts of ctsb, ctsd, and ctsl were also overexpressed. In addition, the RNAs expressions of some genes related to blood-pressure homeostasis and immunovascular responses were identified to be significantly induced by GQDs (Table S1), e.g. the expressions of angiotensinogen (agt), angiotensin-converting enzyme (ace), and kininogen (kng) in GQDs exposed groups were notably higher than those in control, suggesting that both the renin-angiotensin system (RAS) and kinin system were activated to compensate the aggressive blood pressure elicited by GQDs. Moreover, those highly expressed genes might regulated the occurrence of bleeding in zebrafish larvae exposed to GQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs induced AP-1-dependent regulation of il-8 signaling pathway
ce:section.ce:para: Among all up-regulated DEGs, interleukin-8 (il-8) was found to be the most highly activated gene by more than 20 fold in the alterations of transcript levels, compared to control. In most cases, the high expression level of il-8 is an earlier signaling of the aggregation of inflammatory cells (such as macrophages, neutrophils) in lesion regions [41,42], and cd68, f4/80, c3a, c5a, and f-selectin were identified to be significantly induced by GQDs. The high transcriptional expressions of il-8 can be accounted for the coordinated activation of transcription factor AP-1 complex [43]. AP-1 complex included the components of c-fos, jun-b, atf3, and jdp2, which were all remarkably activated more than 2 fold by GQDs, compared to those in control (Table S2a). On the other hand, Iκβɑ, a NF-κβ repressor, was found to be notably induced, whilst the expression levels of all five NF-κβ family members (nfkb1, nfkb2, rela, relb, and rel) were found to be insignificantly changed between GQDs exposed groups and control, suggesting that GQDs mediated induction of il-8 transcription was probably independent of NF-κβ, and then a regulatory balance between AP-1 and NF-κβ activities would occur in GQDs exposed zebrafish, similar to that finding in the control of dendritic cell fate [44].

Il-8 as a chemokine is commonly produced through Toll-like receptor (TLR) signaling pathways [45], but the members of TLR family were not significantly changed in transcriptional levels between GQDs exposed groups and control. Interestingly, zebrafish possesses two tlr4 paralogous, tlr4a and tlr4b [46]. According to the qPCR results, there was no statistically significant difference in the expression levels of tlr4a between GQDs exposed groups and control, while the transcriptional levels of trl4b were notably up-regulated in GQDs exposed groups by more than 2 fold, compared to those in the control (P < 0.01), which was different from the transcriptomic data in this study (Table S2b). Even at the exposure concentration of 50 μg/mL, tlr4b was still remarkably induced by GQDs (Fig. 3 B). Probable reasons for the discrepancy between qPCR and RNA-seq results were the low copy numbers of tlr4 and the subsequent mismatch errors occurred in the process of assembling contigs with very high sequence identity (86%) between tlr4a and tlr4b. To assess whether zebrafish Tlr4 was linked with the intracellular downstream Tlr4 signaling components, the transcriptional levels of genes functioning as TLR folding and TLR-ligand recognition were determined [47], and the results showed that most of the accessory genes including myd88, tram, trif, ticam2 and tril were not stimulated by GQDs, but only a mal-like DEG and traf6 were significantly up-regulated by pka, elk4, map2k4, map3k81.75 fold. In addition, as a potent regulator of tlr4 transcription [48], spi1 (ENSDARG00000000767) was identified to be significantly up-regulated by GQDs (Table S2a).

Similarly, cxcr2, one of the Il-8 receptors, was significantly up-regulated by GQDs. However, the expressions of cxcr1 were examined using qPCR assay, but no significant changes were observed between GQDs exposed group and control. Therefore, Cxcr2 was specifically induced by GQDs to bind with Il-8 for the downstream inflammatory responses. As demonstrated in KEGG enriched pathways, a series of genes in the TNF and MAPK signaling pathways were tightly correlated with the Il-8-mediated signal transduction, such as egfr, pka, elk4, map2k4, map3k8, and traf6, which were significantly regulated by GQDs (Table S2a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs activated the redox-sensitive signaling pathway
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, the redox-sensitive pathway was highly activated by GQDs exposure. Redox enzymes in cytochrome P450 system, including cytochrome P450 (cyp), P450 reductase, and cyclooxygenase-1 (cox-1), cox-2, cox-4, cox-6b, cox-7a, P450 oxidoreductase (pox), cytochrome b561 (cyb561) and cyb5 were up-regulated (Table S3). The activation of these enzymes suggested that a large amount of reactive oxygen species (ROS) were produced in zebrafish larvae after exposed to GQDs. A series of complex cascade of ROS-induced molecular signals associated with oxidative stress, vascular inflammation and remodeling, and cell apoptosis were identified from the DEGs database. Notably, the expressions of hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (hif-1α) were dramatically induced to 2.41 fold among other ROS-responsive genes (Table S3).Since Hif-1α was involved in the direct regulation of downstream vegfa transcription after induced by GQDs, ROS-mediated HIF-1α/Vegfa pathway was probably triggered in response to GQDs induced vascular injury. In addition, casp3, one of the key executioners of apoptosis, was up-regulated by GQDs, and bcl-6 and mcl-1, the downstream Bcl-2 family members, were also activated, which were consistent with the previous reports [35,49]. Some other angiogenic regulators tightly coupled the redox-sensitive pathway, especially six fibroblast growth factors (FGFs), were significantly up-regulated by GQDs (Table S3).

Interestingly, a total of 16 cytochrome P450 genes belonging to 7 different P450 families were notably induced, suggesting that they were involved in the hydroxylation of GQDs. Nearly half of these induced P450 genes were enriched into the Cyp2 family (Table S3). According to the FPKM statistical results, cyp3a27, cyp24a1, and cyp1a were three abundant transcripts in the expression profiles after exposure to GQDs. The transcriptional levels of cyp3a27 were about 3 fold higher than the total expression levels of cyp24a1 and cyp1a. However, cyp2f2 and cyp2 × 12 were the highest induced P450 genes, which were up-regulated by approximate 15 fold and 9 fold by GQDs. To a lesser extent, cyp1a, cyp1c1, and cyp1c2 were up-regulated by 5.7, 3.84, and 2.43 fold, respectively. The expression levels of ahr1b and its repressor ahrr1 were also significantly regulated by GQDs (Fig. 3B), indicating a feedback loop and reciprocal regulation occurred in the xenobiotic metabolisms [50].

To verify the expression profiles of the RNA-seq data, 28 significant DEGs (FDR < 0.05, fold alterations > 1.5) from different signaling pathways were selected for qPCR analysis. The transcriptional levels of these genes were normalized using eflα expression as the internal reference. As was expected, a positive linear relationship was displayed between the qPCR results and RNA-seq data (Fig. 3A), and statistically significant difference was observed between two datasets (R2 = 0.9303, t-test P < 0.01). Accordingly, the data suggested that the RNA-seq results were basically qualified to be positive references for expression profiling analysis, and the quality of the assembled sequences was excellent.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GQDs triggered signaling pathways in a concentration-dependent manner
ce:section.ce:para: qPCR analysis was performed to examine the gene expression changes in zebrafish larvae when exposed to a gradient concentration of GQDs (25-50-100 μg /mL). Results are shown in Fig. 3C, most of the genes from different pathways were significantly up-regulated by the highest exposure concentration of GQDs (100 μg/mL), which were in accordance with those in the RNA-seq results. However, a hierarchical model of concentration-dependent gene expression alterations induced by GQDs was observed between GQDs exposed groups and control. When exposed to a low exposure concentration of GQDs (25 μg/mL), ahr1b and ahrr1 were significantly up-regulated by GQDs in zebrafish, suggesting that AhR signaling pathway underlay the early-response system to neutralize GQDs invading. Moreover, the expression profiles of other isoforms of ahr and ahrr were confirmed by qPCR, and the transcriptional levels of ahr1a, ahr2 and ahrr2 were not significantly changed between GQDs exposed groups and control, which was consistent with the transcriptome data. When exposed to a medium concentration (50 μg/mL) of GQDs, the complement system and redox-sensitive system in zebrafish can be fully or partly activated. Only when zebrafish larvae were treated by the highest concentration of GQDs (100 μg/mL), the coagulation system, RAS system, and HIF-1α/Vegfa pathway can be significantly activated (Fig. 3C).

In order to figure out the possible relevance among various signaling pathways induced by GQDs, we have drafted the gene regulatory network by mapping RNA-seq data against the known database of DNA-protein and protein-protein interactions (Cytoscape 3.5.0 software). Interestingly, hepatocyte nuclear factor 4 alpha (HNF4 A) was identified to be the most universal transcription factor (TF), involving in a series of biological activities from metabolism (cyp1a), inflammatory responses (cd68), to apoptosis (blc-6), and it also acts via ATF2 and HIF-1α to regulate the transcription levels of il-8 and et-1, respectively (Fig.6 ). Our network partly explained the high transcriptional levels of et-1, except HIF-1α, GATA2 and AP-1 are the potential transcriptional regulators of et-1.

As shown in the generalized KEGG pathways and the apoptosis detection, a potential hemorrhage risk occurred in the GQDs exposed zebrafish, because the complement system and RAS system were both excessively activated. Our transcriptome data provided a complementary explanation for the ‘protein corona’ formation between carbon nanomaterials and biomolecules in previous studies, e.g., carbon nanotubes and graphene oxides have been proven to absorb a mixture of proteins, such as complement components, coagulation factors, lipoproteins, and immunoglobulins [51,52]. As such, it can be expected that GQDs can induce large-scale productions of nanoparticle-protein corona in zebrafish, and the NP-protein complex might serve as an upstream stimulator to trigger various signaling pathways.

Our results demonstrated that il-8 transcription levels were solely regulated by the activated AP-1 transcription factor complex and were independent of the canonical Toll-like signaling pathway. It is well known that in mammals TLR4 is the initial sensor in response to lipopolysaccharides (LPS), and directly transactivates downstream NF-κβ factors, thereby causing the release of a relatively wide range of pro-inflammatory cytokines [45], such as IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, and IL-23. However, zebrafish have evolved an ability of high tolerance for LPS, and subsequently the signal transduction of LPS via a Tlr4- and Myd88-independent manner had been well demonstrated in zebrafish [46,53,54]. Similarly, our study displayed that no significant changes were observed in the expression levels of NF-κβ factors between GQDs and controls, but zebrafish tlr4b was specifically induced by GQDs, possibly through its promoter activation by Spi1, which was significantly stimulated in GQDs exposed zebrafish. Considering the important roles of Toll-like receptors in inflammatory signaling pathways and the still unidentified functions of zebrafish Tlr4, it is very meaningful to explore whether zebrafish tlr4b involves in downstream inflammatory pathways. Molecular interconnections between AP-1 and MAPK signals were identified from our network, but their interactive model was not easily delineated by our transcriptome data, in that the transcription activities of AP-1 mostly depend on specific phosphorylation by MAPK signals (e.g. JNK), thereby further work on the relationship between AP-1 and MAPK signaling pathway in GQDs exposed zebrafish should be examined. Interestingly, we found that GQDs -induced apoptosis was probably regulated by AP-1-mediated FasL signaling pathway, which was well demonstrated in previous studies.

It is worth noting that GQDs itself may be partly responsible for ROS production in zebrafish, due to its intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity toward H2O2 [55]. Hence combined with the activated cytochrome P450 system, a significant accumulation of ROS can be generated in GQDs exposed zebrafish. ROS can function as small messenger molecules to modulate the activity of multiple transcription factors and posttranslational hydroxylation of proteins [56]. Compared to the previous study [57], the present findings suggested that AP-1 transcription factor might be induced by GQDs via a ‘ROS-production-mediated’ pathway. If such, a coherent signaling pathway initiated from ROS can be speculated, implying a ‘ROS-AP1-Ii-8′ pathway underlying the GQDs induced inflammatory responses might occur in zebrafish. Our results also pointed out the formation of ‘AhR/AhRR feedback loop’ model induced by GQDs, and some P450 genes were transcriptionally up-regulated in a ‘ligand-activated’ action mode. Among the above generalized pathways, redox-sensitive signaling pathway is of the great importance because ROS can stimulate signaling cascades in response to vasoactive peptides, chemokines, hormones, growth factors, and coagulation factors, as well as to various kinds of physical and chemical irritations [58]. Also, AhR signaling pathway may serve as an auxiliary depot to facilitate the production of ROS through a DNA-binding-dependent manner, to some extent.

In this study, the molecular regulatory networks underlying the apoptosis in zebrafish larvae after exposure to GQDs were revealed by RNA-seq whole transcriptome analysis. In conclusion, this study first illustrates that the molecular regulatory networks are comprised of different signaling pathways triggered by GQDs and provides a new insight into the toxicity mechanisms of GQDs on innate immune systems and metabolic systems in zebrafish larvae. The well-elucidated neutralizing mechanisms of GQDs give supports to the earlier results about the toxic effects of GQDs on in vivo and in vitro systems, and also provide a valuable basis for the toxicity evaluation of other carbon-based nanoparticles. Moreover, our results point to the potential health risks elicited by the high exposure concentration of GQDs, thus heralding an alert for future applications of GQDs in biomedical products.

All zebrafish experiments were performed according to the Guiding Principles for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals, and were approved by the Animal Care Committee of Chongqing Institute of Green and Intelligent Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences (Approval ID: ZKCQY0112).

Firstly, the precursor graphene oxide (GO) was synthesized from graphene powder (XFNano Materials Tech Co., Ltd., Nanjing, China) according to the modified Hummer’s method [59]. Briefly, 0.5 g GO was suspended in 20 mL Dimethyl Formamide (DMF) solution and treated by ultrasonic cell crusher (Scientz-IIF, Ningbo Scientz Biotechnology Co., Ltd., Ningbo, China) for 5 min. After that, the GO/DMF solution was transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave (50 mL) and heated to 200 °C for 5 h. After cooling down the reactor to room temperature, the large size black precipitates were filtered by using a 0.22 μm microporous membrane and the light yellow supernatant was dialyzed with a dialysis bag (100 kDa cutoff) to remove the impurities. The processed solutions were concentrated and rotary evaporated, the final GQDs were acquired at a concentration of 6 mg/mL.

Zebrafish wild-type AB strains were reared at 28 °C and a constant photoperiod of 14:10 h (light: dark) in a conventional way as described by Westerfield [60]. Zebrafish for oviposition were 4 months old, and embryos for exposure experiment were harvested at 4 h post-fertilization (hpf). Normally developed embryos were identified by a dissecting light microscope (SZ760 OPTEC, Chongqing, China) and pooled for GQDs immersion assays. Fifty embryos (4 hpf) were carefully transferred into each well of six-well plates and supplemented with aqueous solutions of GQDs at various concentrations (0, 25, 50 and 100 μg/mL), at least three replicate trials were performed for each concentration group. The newly-emerged zebrafish were continuously exposed to GQDs for 7 days, after that all zebrafish samples were collected after anesthesia with tricaine methane sulfonate (MS222, 168 mg/L) followed by rapid freezing in liquid nitrogen, and subsequently stored at −80 °C until RNA extraction for transcriptome analysis and qPCR.

ce:section.ce:section-title: RNA extraction, library preparation and Illumina Hiseq4000 sequencing
ce:section.ce:para: Total RNA of GQDs exposed zebrafish at the concentration of 100 μg/mL and the control was extracted using RNAiso plus according to the manufacture’s protocol (Takara, Dalian, China) and genomic DNA was removed using DNase I (Takara, Dalian, China). RNA quality was determined using the Agilent 2100 Bioanalyser system (Agilent, USA) and quantified using the NanoDrop ND-2000 (Thermo Scientific, USA). High-quality RNA samples (OD260/280 = 1.8∼2.2, OD260/230 ≥ 2.0, RIN ≥ 6.5, 28S:18S≥1.0, >10 μg) were used to construct the sequencing library. RNA-seq transcriptome libraries were prepared following TruSeqTM RNA sample preparation Kit from Illumina (San Diego, CA, USA), and 5 μg of total RNA was prepared. Subsequently, mRNA was isolated with polyA selection by oligo (dT) beads and fragmented using fragmentation buffer. cDNA synthesis, end repair, A-base addition and ligation of the Illumina-indexed adaptors were performed according to Illumina’s protocol. Libraries were then size selected for cDNA target fragments followed by PCR amplified using Phusion DNA polymerase (NEB, England, UK) for 15 PCR cycles. After quantified by TBS380, Paired-end libraries were sequenced with the Illumina HiSeq (2 × 150 bp read length).

The raw paired end reads were trimmed and quality controlled by SeqPrep and Sickle with default parameters. Then clean reads were separately aligned to reference genome with orientation mode using Bowtie2 software [61]. The mapping criteria of Bowtie were as follows: sequencing reads should be uniquely matched to the genome allowing up to 2 mismatches, without insertions or deletions. The regions of the gene were expanded following depths of sites and the operon was obtained. In addition, the whole genome was split into multiple 15 kb windows that share 5 kb. New transcribed regions were defined as more than 2 consecutive windows without the overlapped region of gene, where at least 2 reads mapped per window in the same orientation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Differential expression analysis and functional enrichment
ce:section.ce:para: To identify DEGs (differential expression genes) between the two different samples, the expression level for each transcript was calculated using the fragments per kilobase of exon per million mapped reads (FRKM) method. Cuffdiff was used for the preliminary differential expression analysis [62], and the DE-seq analysis by R software was further introduced to screen more DEGs. DEGs between the GQDs treatments and controls were identified using the following criteria: (1) the logarithmic of fold change was greater than 1.5, (2) the FDR should be less than 0.05. To understand the functions of the differentially expressed genes, gene ontology (GO) functional enrichment and KEGG pathway analysis were carried out by Goatools and KOBAS softwares [63]. DEGs were significantly enriched in GO terms and metabolic pathways when their Bonferroni-corrected P-value was less than 0.05.

To validate the RNA-seq results, quantitative real-time PCR (qPCR) was performed using ABI ViiA7™ real-time PCR system (Applied Biosystems, Carlsbad, CA, USA) with SYBR® Premix Ex Taq TM II (Perfect Real Time, Takara). Zebrafish were exposed to 25, 50, and 100 μg/mL of GQDs for subsequent RNA extraction and cDNA synthesis. The first-strand cDNAs were transcribed with PrimeScript™ RT reagent kit with gDNA eraser (Takara, Dalian, China) according to the manufacturer's instructions, and 1 μg of the total mRNA was prepared for the cDNA synthesis. Running program was initiated from 95 °C, incubating for 5 min, followed by 40 cycles of 95 °C for 15 s, 60 °C for 30 s, 72 °C for 30 s. Specific primers for selected genes were designed by Beacon Design 8 and several pairs of primer sequences were available referred from earlier published data (Table 1). egfɑ was selected as the internal control normalized the relative mRNA levels, three independent biological replicates were performed for each treatment, and 2−ΔΔCt method was used to quantify gene expression levels in the experiment. Statistical analysis was carried out with SPSS 19.0 software; significant differences were determined by independent sample t-test. The levels of p < 0.05 were considered to be significant.

To figure out the relationship among various signaling pathways, we integrated GQDs induced genes that were functionally classified into those significantly enriched pathways. Molecular data were processed using the online String tools, and the final gene interactive map was generated by exporting the String data into Cytoscape (ver. 3.4.0).

Zebrafish embryos were exposed to GQDs for 48 h, and washed three times with 1 × PBS before transferred to 6-well plates for AO/EB staining. Before dyeing, embryos were dechorionated using 2 mg/mL protease E. Acridine orange (AO) and ethidium bromide (EB) were prepared to uniformly mix with the final concentration of 0.5 mg/mL, and embryos were immersed in AO/EB solution for 30 min at 28℃ and covered with silver papers. After washing three times with 1 × PBS, embryos were carefully pipetted into glass slides and observed with confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM). The number of apoptotic cells represented by orange-red fluorescent colors was counted by Image-Pro Plus 6.0 software.

ROS levels were detected in zebrafish embryos using (2′, 7′-dichlorodihydroﬂuorescein diacetate) DCF-DA according to the previously reported methods [77]. In brief, 35 embryos per group at 72 hpf were chosen for ROS quantification, each concentration was performed for at least three biological replicates. The fluorescence was automatically recorded by Infinite M200 Pro (Tecan, Switzerland). The excitation wavelength was set as 480 nm, and the emission wavelength was 515 nm.
Statistical analysis was carried out with SPSS 19.0 software; significant differences were determined by independent sample t-test. The levels of p < 0.05 were considered to be significant.
The authors declare no conflict of interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful for the supports from the Three Hundred Leading Talents in Scientific and Technological Innovation Program of Chongqing (No. CSTCCXLJRC201714 to D.S.P.), the Key Application and Development Program of Chongqing Science and Technology Commission (No. cstc2014yykfC20004 and cstc2014yykfC20002 to D.S.P), the Hundred Talents Program of Chinese Academy of Sciences (to D.S.P.), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21407146 to Y.B.M. and No. 51641907 to A.P.N).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jhazmat.2018.05.063.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
